retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Water Balance -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Interstitial fluid volume—This cannot be measured directly but is calculated by subtracting PV from ECFV, as the latter 2 variables are measurable.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Water Balance -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Much of this information can appear abstract, especially when talking about compartments that are more of a theoretical space. Therefore, it is crucial to have a way to measure the volumes of the different compartments physically. The way to measure the different spaces is by using the indicator-dilution method. [9] The theory behind this is that to measure the volume of a specific compartment; one must introduce measurable substances into the body that are distributed uniformly to a compartment of interest. Using this method, individual volumes can be measured directly, and others can be measured by subtracting the volumes of related compartments. This information can then be quantified by using the equation Volume (V) = Amount (substance injected)/Concentration (measured after equilibration). [10] The following compartments can be measured as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Water Balance -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Extracellular fluid volume (ECFV)—To measure this volume, labeled inulin, sucrose, mannitol, or sulfate can be injected. These large molecules are impermeable to the cell membrane and only diffuse to the plasma and interstitial spaces.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Water Balance -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Intracellular fluid volume—This cannot be measured directly but can be calculated by subtracting ECFV from TBW, as the latter 2 variables are measurable.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Colloid Osmotic Pressure -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: One method of direct measurement of interstitial colloid osmotic pressure is the wick method, [4] which involves the sampling of interstitial COP with multifilamentous nylon wicks, which are first washed and soaked in priming solution before being sewn into the subcutaneous tissue of an animal being studies. After a certain period, the wicks are pulled out, and the wick fluid isolated by centrifugation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Colloid Osmotic Pressure -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Another method of measurement called the crossover method involves priming the wicks in several different solutions of various concentrations. COP in the fluid within the wick increases during implantation only in wicks primed with fluid with lower protein concentration than the ISF. By plotting the COP of the priming fluid against the COP of wick fluid after implantation, a linear plot can be constructed with the crossing point of the two representing the true COP of the interstitium. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Crystalloid Fluids -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: A crystalloid fluid is an aqueous solution of mineral salts and other small, water-soluble molecules. Most commercially available crystalloid solutions are isotonic to human plasma. These fluids approximate concentrations of various solutes found in plasma and do not exert an osmotic effect in vivo. Crystalloid fluids function to expand intravascular volume without disturbing ion concentration or causing significant fluid shifts between intracellular, intravascular, and interstitial spaces.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Water Balance -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: At a cellular level, the distribution of the various fluid compartments in the body is paramount for maintaining health, function, and survival. For the average 70 kg man, 60% of the total body weight is comprised of water, equaling 42 L. The body's fluid separates into 2 main compartments: intracellular fluid volume (ICFV) and extracellular fluid volume (ECFV). Of the 42 L of water in the body, two-thirds is within the intracellular fluid (ICF) space, which equates to 28 L. The ECFV comprises 2 spaces: The interstitial fluid volume (ISFV) and the plasma volume (PV). One-third of the total body water is the ECFV, equivalent to 14 L. Out of the extracellular fluid volume, 75% or 10.5L of the volume is present in the interstitial space, and 25% of that water is in the plasma, equivalent to 3.5 L. [3] Each space works in unison with the others and has different functions that are paramount for normal physiological function.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Blood Plasma -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Water constitutes about two-thirds of the human body. In an adult man weighing 70 kg, the body water content is about 42L. This water content is divided into two major compartments: Intracellular fluid (ICF): It forms about 28 L (about 40% of total body weight) Extracellular fluid (ECF): It forms about 14 L (about 20% of total body weight), of which 15% is interstitial fluid and 5% is plasma

retrieved_docs.title: Hypovolemia(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hypovolemia refers to a state of low extracellular fluid volume, generally secondary to combined sodium and water loss. All living organisms must maintain an adequate fluid balance to preserve homeostasis. Water constitutes the most abundant fluid in the body, at around 50% to 60% of the body weight. Total body water is further divided into the intracellular fluid (ICF), which comprises 55% to 75%, and the extracellular fluid (ECF), which comprises around 25-45%. The ECF is further divided into the intravascular and extravascular (interstitial) spaces. ECF is the more readily measured component as it can be estimated by arterial blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Insensible Fluid Loss -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of intravascular volume and clinical assessment are critical because fluid compartment volumes cannot easily be measured. Intravascular volume may undergo assessment by using laboratory/physical exams, and hemodynamic monitoring means.  Frequent evaluations are necessary despite the method used to confirm the initial impression to best manage fluid replacement. The physical examination is most reliable when assessing a patient’s baseline fluid status . [9] Clues to hypovolemia include hydration of mucous membranes, skin turgor, resting heart rate, and intensity of peripheral pulses. Additionally, blood pressure particularly orthostatic changes and urinary output, are also important indicators of fluid status gleaned from a physical examination. Perioperatively, physiologic manipulation from surgical stress and anesthesia alter these signs (due to increased antidiuretic hormone secretion) rendering them unreliable. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Edema -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The average human is made up of between 50 to 60 percent water. Total body water divides into two main compartments: intracellular and extracellular comprising two-thirds and one-third total body water, respectively. Of these compartments, the extracellular space is subdivided into two additional categories: interstitial and intravascular, making up sixty and forty percent of extracellular space, respectively. Fluid maintenance in the human body is a delicate balance of fluid intake and output. The interstitial fluid, the fluid between cells, is derived from capillaries with a similar solute content to plasma except for protein content. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Water Balance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, there is a relationship between the interstitial fluid and intracellular fluid. These 2 environments very closely influence each other as the cell's membrane separates them. Generally, nutrients diffuse into the cell, with waste products entering the interstitial space. Ions are typically barred from crossing the membrane but can occasionally cross via active transport or under specific conditions. Water can move freely across the membrane and is directed by the osmotic gradient between the 2 spaces. Changes in the intracellular fluid volume result from alterations in the osmolarity of the ECF but do not respond to isosmotic changes in extracellular volume. [8] However, any water flow in or out of the cell membrane has proportional changes in the ECFV. If a disturbance causes ECF osmolarity to increase, water flows out of the cell and into the extracellular space to balance the osmotic gradient; however, the total body osmolarity remains higher than what is typical, and the cell shrinks. If a disturbance were to cause a decrease in ECF osmolarity, then water would move from the ECF into the ICF to attain an osmolar equilibrium; however, the total body osmolarity would remain lower than normal, and the cell would swell. Third, if isosmotic fluid entered the extracellular space, then there would be no net changes in the ICF, and the ECFV would increase.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Body Fluids -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Where Jv is the net rate of capillary fluid movement, Kfc is a capillary filtration fluid coefficient, Pc is capillary hydrostatic pressure, Pi is interstitial hydrostatic pressure, n is the osmotic reflection coefficient, Op is plasma oncotic pressure, and Oi is interstitial oncotic pressure. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with the development of acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). In turn, acute kidney injury is clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the importance of adequate intravascular volume in preserving renal function. Acute kidney injury also encompasses the condition of extracellular blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that disturbances in nitrogenous waste handling outside the kidneys are a relevant component of its clinical spectrum. Understanding these interconnections—how dehydration can precipitate acute kidney injury, how hypovolemia contributes to its progression, and how extracellular nitrogen abnormalities manifest—helps clinicians identify risk factors, monitor patients, and implement timely interventions to protect renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 782
retrieved_docs.content: Blood and related products include a subcategory called blood substitutes and perfusion solutions. Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions, in turn, encompass IV solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients. Within this group of IV solutions, there are specific formulations identified as solutions producing osmotic diuresis. Thus, solutions producing osmotic diuresis are a specialized type of IV solution used for parenteral delivery of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients, and these IV solutions are considered part of the broader class of blood substitutes and perfusion solutions that belong to the overall group of blood and related products. This hierarchical organization helps clinicians understand how various fluid therapies relate to one another and supports appropriate selection of products for volume replacement, electrolyte management, or targeted diuretic effect in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1566
retrieved_docs.content: Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance and solutions producing osmotic diuresis are both subcategories of iv solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients. As iv solutions, they are administered directly into the bloodstream to provide essential fluid volume, correct electrolyte disturbances, and deliver nutritional substrates. Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance are specifically formulated to modify the concentrations of key ions such as sodium, potassium, calcium, and magnesium, thereby helping to restore or maintain proper electrolyte homeostasis in patients with imbalances. Solutions producing osmotic diuresis contain solutes that create an osmotic gradient in the renal tubules, promoting increased urine output to eliminate excess fluid and certain electrolytes, which can be useful in conditions requiring fluid removal or reduction of edema. Together, these iv solution types are integral components of parenteral therapy, enabling clinicians to tailor fluid, electrolyte, and nutritional management to the individual needs of patients in acute or chronic care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2962
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2) and with reduced blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Type 2 diabetes can predispose patients to acute kidney injury through chronic hyperglycemia‑induced vascular and tubular damage, making the kidneys more vulnerable to hemodynamic stress. Conversely, hypovolemia decreases renal perfusion and can directly precipitate acute kidney failure. Recognizing that both type 2 diabetes and low intravascular volume are important clinical contributors to acute kidney failure guides clinicians to monitor diabetic patients closely, ensure adequate hydration, and intervene promptly to preserve renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1450
retrieved_docs.content: B-type natriuretic peptide is classified as a natriuretic substance, meaning it belongs to the broader group of natriuretic peptides that regulate fluid balance and vascular tone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: irrigating solutions and electrolyte‑balancing formulations are organized within a unified pharmacological framework, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate intravenous therapies for fluid resuscitation, nutrition, electrolyte management, antifibrinolytic treatment and metabolic modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما) is clinically associated with high blood glucose (ارتفاع السكر في الدم), indicating that hyperglycemia often contributes to or results from elevated osmotic concentration in the blood. This condition is also linked to urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), suggesting that disturbances of the urinary system can be an underlying or related factor in the development of high plasma osmolality. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the close interplay between osmotic balance, fluid volume status, and glucose regulation. Recognizing that hyperglycemia, urinary tract pathology, and hypovolemia commonly intersect with elevated plasma osmolality helps clinicians assess underlying causes and guide comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperosmolality and hypernatriemia are classified as part of the broader category of other disorders of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance, reflecting their role as disturbances in plasma osmolality and sodium concentration that impact overall homeostatic regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1852
retrieved_docs.content: Solutions producing osmotic diuresis are a specific type of IV solution used in the parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. By creating an osmotic gradient that draws water into the renal tubules, these solutions promote diuresis, helping to manage fluid balance, correct electrolyte disturbances, and support nutritional needs in patients requiring intravenous therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 39
retrieved_docs.content: clarifies how sodium‑based compounds, amino‑acid additives, antifibrinolytic agents, and irrigating solutions are interrelated and guides clinicians in selecting appropriate intravenous or irrigating formulations for fluid, electrolyte, and nutrient therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2548
retrieved_docs.content: Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance are intravenous solutions used in the parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 629
retrieved_docs.content: BLOOD SUBSTITUTES AND PERFUSION SOLUTIONS is a broad therapeutic category that encompasses both irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion and iv solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients. Within the irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion group, the subclass Other irrigating solutions in ATC is defined, and this subclass is noted to contain the compound glycin. The iv solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients also include a specific subclass, Solutions producing osmotic diuresis, which are formulated to induce osmotic diuresis. Additionally, BLOOD SUBSTITUTES AND PERFUSION SOLUTIONS is considered part of the larger group of blood and related products, linking it to other blood‑derived therapeutics. These hierarchical relationships clarify how various fluid and perfusion products are organized within pharmacological classifications and how specific formulations, such as those containing glycin or designed for osmotic diuresis, fit into the overall therapeutic landscape.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2237
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions constitute a specific category of intravenous (IV) solutions that are administered parenterally to deliver fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. As a subclass of IV solutions used for parenteral administration, these products are formulated to replace or augment blood components, provide rapid volume expansion, and supply essential nutrients when oral intake is not possible. They play a critical role in clinical settings such as fluid resuscitation for hypovolemia, management of blood loss, and provision of nutritional support for patients who require intravenous therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with dehydration (التجفاف) and with abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). When renal function declines sharply, the condition can give rise to extrarenal blood nitrogen accumulation (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), reflecting the buildup of nitrogenous waste products that the kidneys are no longer able to clear. These interrelated clinical features highlight the importance of recognizing fluid deficits and neuropsychiatric changes as potential indicators of acute kidney injury and of monitoring nitrogen waste levels to guide timely management.
